summary: Nadia Asparouhova examines Arizona State University's radical transformation under President Michael Crow as a model for reimagining higher education. Rather than competing with Ivy League schools through exclusivity, ASU measures success by whom they include and how those students succeed. The university reorganized into three pillars—Academic Enterprise (degrees), Knowledge Enterprise (research), and Learning Enterprise (lifelong education)—while dramatically increasing enrollment, research output, and graduation rates. ASU achieved top-100 research university status while maintaining an 86% acceptance rate and significantly expanding access to underrepresented students. Asparouhova argues this model demonstrates that prestige need not correlate with exclusivity, and that academia and tech share fundamental values around spreading great ideas.
key_points: ASU rejected the 'Harvardization' playbook of establishing prestige through exclusivity, instead measuring success by inclusion and student outcomes
The university increased enrollment from 55,000 to 76,000 students while simultaneously improving four-year graduation rates by over 20 percentage points
ASU's 'use-inspired research' philosophy emphasizes pragmatic, actionable knowledge that addresses real-world problems rather than pure academic inquiry
The three-pillar organizational structure (Academic, Knowledge, Learning Enterprise) separates the incompatible business models typically bundled together in universities
Despite tech's critique of academia, universities remain civilization's most refined structure for sustained knowledge production and deserve reform rather than abandonment
money_quotes: ASU is a comprehensive public research university, measured not by whom we exclude, but rather by whom we include and how they succeed.
People in tech actually care about making ideas happen, not just talking about them.

Academic social networks are some of the most refined and valuable structures our civilization has produced. Nowhere have we amassed a greater concentration of specialists focused full-time on knowledge production.
It's as if hungry customers were banging down the door of the French Laundry, whose owners throw up their hands and say 'Sorry, we don't have any reservations available!'
Here, the only mistake a dean can make, the biggest mistake, is not taking risks.
A cohort with these academic qualifications comprises more freshmen from the top decile than the total number of students in the freshman class of Harvard University.
Prestige doesn't have to correlate to exclusivity; longstanding institutions are capable of reinventing themselves.
key_insight: The best universities of the future will be measured not by whom they exclude but by whom they include and how they succeed—proving that scale and quality need not be at odds.
